metadata.title: Guideline on immunogenicity assessment of monoclonal antibodies intended for in vivo clinical use
metadata.note: Addendum to 'Guideline on Immunogenicity Assessment of Biotechnology-Derived Therapeutic Proteins' (EMEA/CHMP/BMWP/14327/2006) — must be read in conjunction. Covers mAbs, their derivatives (Fab fragments, scFv, nanobodies, minibodies), conjugates, and Fc-linked fusion proteins. Aimed at MAA stage but principles apply to earlier development phases.

rules.rule_text: Standard immunoglobulin-detecting reagents (anti-immunoglobulins, Protein A, Protein G) are inappropriate for detecting antibodies against mAbs because they bind to the mAb product itself; applicants must use alternative assay formats (e.g. bridging ELISA, ECL, or SPR) and must justify the suitability of the chosen approach, taking full consideration of limitations of each method.
rules.verbatim_quote: Many standard assay formats involve the use of anti-immunoglobulin reagents such as antibodies against immunoglobulins, protein A or protein G, but these are inappropriate for use in detecting antibodies against mAbs as they very often bind to the product itself. [...] Applicants need to justify the suitability of the chosen approach, taking into full consideration the limitations of the respective methods.

rules.rule_text: Immunogenicity assays for mAbs must be tailored to reduce matrix-related artefacts (from complement components, mannose binding protein, Fc receptors, soluble target molecules, complement receptor 1, rheumatoid factors, and residual product) to achieve acceptable background signal levels, sensitivity, and specificity.
rules.verbatim_quote: Samples (normally serum or plasma) may contain substances that interfere with the assays i.e. matrix effects which produce false positive or negative results and/or incorrect assessment of antibody content. [...] Assays often need to be 'tailored' to reduce artefacts and achieve acceptable background signal levels, sensitivity and specificity.

rules.rule_text: Assay design must specifically address interference from residual circulating mAb product in samples, which causes artefactually low (false negative) anti-drug antibody estimates; acceptable approaches include: delayed sampling, use of ECL-based assays, acid dissociation of complexes prior to detection, or sample dilution (with recognition of sensitivity trade-off). In many cases a combination of all three approaches is required.
rules.verbatim_quote: This normally results in an artefactually low estimate of antibody content of affected samples and can be so pronounced as to cause false negative results. Several approaches have been proposed to overcome this problem. [...] In many cases, anti-mAb method development, validation and testing utilizes a combination of all three approaches to reduce product interference.
rules.exceptions: Delayed sampling may fail to detect immunogenicity as induced antibodies may have declined to undetectable levels; each approach must be carefully evaluated.

rules.rule_text: A confirmatory assay must be selected and used after the screening assay; the confirmatory assay must take into account the characteristics of the screening assay. Use of Protein A and Protein G may be appropriate in confirmatory assays to demonstrate that the positive response is due to an immunoglobulin.
rules.verbatim_quote: Confirmatory assays can suffer from the same problems as screening assays. It is important to select an appropriate confirmatory assay taking account of the characteristics of the screening assay. Use of Protein A and Protein G may be appropriate in confirmatory assays to demonstrate that the positive response is due to an immunoglobulin; however other approaches can be used for this.
rules.exceptions: Other approaches may be used to confirm immunoglobulin specificity.

rules.rule_text: Generation of appropriate positive control sera is a critical requirement for all mAb immunogenicity studies; the positive control must be used to monitor assay sensitivity and specificity. When human sera are unavailable in early development, non-human primate sera may serve as positive controls (with recognised limitations regarding non-CDR specificity).
rules.verbatim_quote: Generation of positive control sera is in general a critical issue for immunogenicity studies for mAbs. The chosen positive control serum or purified antibody is important for monitoring assay sensitivity and specificity. If human sera are not available (as is possible during early phases of product development) then use of animal sera is the only option.
rules.exceptions: Anti-idiotypic antiserum or mAb can, in some cases, provide a useful positive control.

rules.rule_text: The neutralising capacity of any antibodies induced against a mAb must normally be measured; any deviations from this requirement must be justified.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is normally expected that the neutralizing capacity of any antibodies induced will be measured. Any deviations from this need to be justified.

rules.rule_text: A thorough biological characterisation of the mAb must be undertaken using appropriate biological and immunological assays before selecting the neutralising assay strategy; properties of the mAb must be assessed to allow selection of an appropriate neutralising assay.
rules.verbatim_quote: In such cases a thorough biological characterization of the mAb must be undertaken, using appropriate biological and immunological assays. Following this, the properties of the mAb need to be assessed to allow selection of an appropriate neutralizing assay strategy.

rules.rule_text: For mAbs, competitive ligand binding assays may be the preferred neutralising assay format (over classic bioassays) because induced antibodies that block mAb binding to its target are most associated with reduced clinical efficacy; however, the choice must be informed by thorough mAb characterisation.
rules.verbatim_quote: Therefore, competitive ligand binding assays may be the neutralizing assays of choice for mAbs rather than classic bioassays. This distinguishes mAbs from other classes of biologicals with regard to immunogenicity assessment.
rules.exceptions: When Fc-mediated immunobiological effects contribute to efficacy, care must be taken not to assume that simple antigen binding is the only mechanism; cell-based assays for measuring neutralisation may have an advantage in these cases.

rules.rule_text: Standard aspects of immunogenicity as described in the general guideline (EMEA/CHMP/BMWP/14327/2006) must be addressed for every new therapeutic mAb, taking into account its specific characteristics, the nature of its intended use, and its therapeutic indication.
rules.verbatim_quote: Standard aspects of immunogenicity as described in the general guideline should be addressed for every new therapeutic mAb, taking into account its characteristics, the nature of the intended use and the therapeutic indication.

rules.rule_text: Applicants must always present a thorough risk identification that takes full account of the nature of the mAb product and its intended use; the standard immunogenicity testing programme may be reduced with thorough justification, or intensified, depending on the level of risk identified.
rules.verbatim_quote: The applicant should always present a thorough risk identification that takes full account of the nature of the product along with to its intended use. Based on a risk identification and assessment strategy as further described below, the standard immunogenicity testing programme may be reduced with thorough justification, or may need to be intensified, depending on the level of risk identified.

rules.rule_text: Prior knowledge about similar mAbs (same target class, expression system) is a required risk identification consideration; where detection methodology for anti-mAb antibodies or their clinical consequences is not sufficiently sensitive, more extensive monitoring of anti-mAb response dynamics relative to therapeutic outcome may have to be considered.
rules.verbatim_quote: Available knowledge or lack of knowledge concerning other similar mAbs (e.g., from the same target class, expressed in the same expression system) is an important consideration. The risk perception may be higher if the methodology to either detect antibodies against mAbs or to detect clinical consequences (e.g. mAb trough concentration, PD parameters and response to mAb treatment) of antibodies against mAbs is not sufficiently sensitive.

rules.rule_text: The mAb structural characteristics (heterologous/rodent sequence, chimeric, humanized, or human sequence) must be evaluated as part of immunogenicity risk identification; next-generation constructs (bispecific mAbs, mAb fragments) require special consideration for their capacity to expose hidden antigenic determinants.
rules.verbatim_quote: Special consideration should be given to next generation products, for example, bispecific mAbs or mAb fragments and their capacity to expose hidden antigenic determinants.

rules.rule_text: Glycosylation patterns of mAbs must be assessed as part of immunogenicity risk identification; non-typical glycosylation patterns (particularly from novel expression systems) may increase immunogenicity risk and must be appropriately identified.
rules.verbatim_quote: Altered glycosylation patterns may decrease or increase the immunogenic properties of the molecule (e.g. change in shielding of the protein backbone). Non-typical glycosylation patterns, e.g. as encountered when adopting entirely novel expression systems, may introduce a higher immunogenicity risk as compared to more commonly used expression systems.

rules.rule_text: Manufacturing process impurities and other product quality attributes that influence immunogenicity must be appropriately identified as part of risk assessment; the analytical and clinical approach to assess, characterize, and mitigate such risks may need to be extensive, and product quality-related risk must be identified.
rules.verbatim_quote: Other factors that influence immunogenicity include impurities arising from the production process and other quality attributes. Therefore, the analytical and clinical approach to assess, characterize and potentially mitigate such potential risks may have to be more extensive, and the risk related to the product quality should be appropriately identified.

rules.rule_text: The mechanism of action of the mAb (cytolytic, apoptotic) and the nature of the target molecule (immunosilencing, immunostimulating) must be adequately characterized and comprehensively investigated as part of immunogenicity risk identification; agonistic mAbs or those where cross-linking could theoretically lead to immunoactivation require careful observation of patients in early clinical trials.
rules.verbatim_quote: The mode of action of the mAb (e.g., cytolytic, apoptotic), and especially the nature of the target molecule (e.g., immunosilencing, immunostimulating), needs to be adequately characterized and comprehensively investigated. [...] For agonistic mAbs or for mAbs where cross-linking could on theoretical considerations lead to immunoactivation, applicants should consider careful observation of patients in early clinical trials to see if such events occur.

rules.rule_text: Clinical factors affecting mAb immunogenicity risk must be considered, including: age-related protein turnover differences (e.g. children vs. adults), prior exposure to similar mAbs, and dosing schedule (intermittent dosing regimens have a higher likelihood of inducing immunogenicity than scheduled/repeated dosing).
rules.verbatim_quote: Immunogenicity for mAbs can be age related e.g. protein turnover is different in children compared to adults and this can result in differences in observed immunogenicity, e.g. for antibodies used in treatment of juvenile arthritis compared to rheumatoid arthritis at comparable doses. Previous exposure to similar or related monoclonal antibodies can also influence immunogenicity. Therapeutic antibodies used with intermittent dosing schemes have a higher likelihood of inducing immunogenicity than when used in a scheduled and repeated dosing scheme.

rules.rule_text: Risk assessment for mAb immunogenicity must take a multidisciplinary approach accounting for product quality control strategy (product formulation, acceptance limits for product-related and process-related impurities); overall risk assessment must be linked to any comparability exercises performed during development when changes to the mAb occur.
rules.verbatim_quote: The assessment of risk requires a multidisciplinary approach taking into account all risks identified (e.g. related to product quality control strategy, including product formulation, justification of acceptance limits for product-related variants and process-related impurities). This also implies that the overall risk assessment should be linked to any comparability exercise potentially performed during development, in case changes to the mAb used occur at different stages of product development.

rules.rule_text: At the time of applying for marketing authorisation, sufficient data must be presented to estimate the severity, rate of occurrence, and detectability of immunogenicity risks; applicants must justify and discuss their overall concept for the design and extent of immunogenicity testing used during the development programme.
rules.verbatim_quote: As a general guiding principle, sufficient data to estimate the severity, rate of occurrence and detectability of the risks should be presented at the time of applying for a marketing authorisation. [...] At the time of marketing authorisation application, applicants should thoroughly justify and discuss their overall concept for the design and extent of immunogenicity testing used during their development programme.

rules.rule_text: Immunogenicity risks identified during risk assessment may require further substantiation by post-marketing surveillance and monitoring.
rules.verbatim_quote: These risks may have to be further substantiated by post-marketing surveillance and monitoring as required.

rules.rule_text: The immunogenicity risk monitoring concept must be planned early in product development and revisited and updated regularly throughout product development and the entire mAb product lifecycle as new data become available.
rules.verbatim_quote: Following such an approach of risk identification and assessment, applicants should carefully plan this concept early in product development, and revisit and update it regularly during product development and the entire lifecycle of the mAb product as new data become available.

rules.rule_text: When a mAb product claims a particular advantage regarding immunogenic potential (e.g. an SmPC claim of low immunogenicity), appropriate data to support this claim is usually required.
rules.verbatim_quote: For products that are claimed to exhibit a particular advantage as regards immunogenic potential, (e.g. a claim in the Summary of Product Characteristics) appropriate data to support this is usually required.

rules.rule_text: IgE testing prior to clinical administration must be considered for patients if the mAb contains non-human carbohydrate structures (e.g. Galactose-α-1,3-Galactose) to prevent severe anaphylaxis; IgE testing should also be considered when there was a high incidence of allergic reactions on first administration during early clinical development.
rules.verbatim_quote: in some instances, IgE testing prior to clinical administration needs to be considered for patients if the mAb contains non-human carbohydrate structures, e.g. Galactose-α-1,3-Galactose, in order to prevent severe anaphylaxis. Another instance where development of IgE testing should be considered is where the incidence of allergic reactions was high on first administration during early clinical development of the product.
rules.exceptions: IgG subclasses and other Ig classes (e.g. IgA) are not a standard requirement but may become necessary with certain identified risks (e.g. nasal application).

rules.rule_text: The determination of neutralising capacity AND the determination of transient versus persistent antibodies by repeated sampling is usually required in mAb immunogenicity programmes.
rules.verbatim_quote: However, the determination of neutralizing capacity and of transient versus persistent mAbs by repeated sampling is usually required.

rules.rule_text: The frequency and timing of antibody sampling must vary based on the identified relative risk level: for low-risk mAbs, reduced sampling frequency in later stages of development is acceptable with justification, provided no adverse events or reduced efficacy have been observed; for high-risk mAbs, more frequent sampling throughout the entire clinical development is required.
rules.verbatim_quote: The frequency and timing of sampling and analysis may vary depending on the identified relative risk level. For lower risk mAbs it may be possible to reduce sampling frequency in later stages of development, provided that no adverse events or reduced efficacy has been observed. [...] For mAbs where a higher risk is determined, sampling may have to be more frequent during the whole clinical development.
rules.exceptions: Sample banking must be undertaken on a routine basis over the whole development programme regardless of risk level.

rules.rule_text: For high-risk mAbs, antibody levels, PK parameters, PD markers, efficacy, and safety must be measured simultaneously and over repeated treatments to assess the clinical significance of antibody development and whether antibody effects change over time (titre increase, isotype switching, affinity maturation).
rules.verbatim_quote: It may be necessary during the clinical development to measure antibody levels, PK, PD markers, efficacy and safety simultaneously and over a period of repeated treatments. This allows assessment of the clinical significance of antibody development, and also whether the antibody effect changes over time, which could occur as a result of increase in titre and/or isotype switching/affinity maturation of the antibody response.

rules.rule_text: Non-neutralising anti-mAb antibodies must also be monitored as they can indirectly impact efficacy by binding to the mAb product and impacting its pharmacokinetic properties; PK parameter measurement is a potential help when planning how to measure anti-mAb antibody responses.
rules.verbatim_quote: Non-neutralizing anti-mAb antibodies may indirectly impact efficacy by binding to the mAb product and impacting on its pharmacokinetic properties. Therefore, measurement of PK parameters may be a potential help when planning how to measure an anti-mAb antibody response.

rules.rule_text: Early immune response indicators should be incorporated where early identification and potential treatment discontinuation is part of the risk mitigation plan: low-affinity IgM development indicates early immune responses; detection of binding non-neutralising antibodies may indicate later development of neutralising antibodies.
rules.verbatim_quote: low affinity IgM antibodies can be an indicator of an early immune response, and measuring IgM responses could help in early identification of patients mounting an immune response. Likewise, the detection of binding, non-neutralizing antibodies may be an early indication of later development of neutralizing antibodies.
rules.exceptions: This applies when risk identification and assessment has concluded that early identification of an immune response is required.

rules.rule_text: At MAA filing, applicants must present an integrated summary of their immunogenicity strategy covering: risk identification, characterisation, monitoring, minimization, and mitigation; this risk-based approach must also be incorporated into the Risk Management Plan (RMP), addressing how identified risks and missing information will be handled post-authorisation.
rules.verbatim_quote: When filing a Marketing Authorisation Application, it is recommended that applicants present an integrated summary of their strategy on risk identification, characterisation, monitoring, minimization and mitigation. This risk-based approach should also have been taken into account for the Risk Management Plan (RMP), discussing how risks identified by data from the development programme, and potential risks, or missing information should be handled in a post-authorisation setting.
